coredata.dc:title: Designing an intriguingly fluorescent N, B-doped carbon dots based fluorescent probe for selective detection of NO2 − ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Linlin
Jana, Jayasmita
Chung, Jin Suk
Choi, Won Mook
Hur, Seung Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Low-cost nitrogen and boron-doped carbon nanodots (CPAP-CDs) with a high quantum yield (64.07%) were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal treatment. The obtained CPAP-CDs exhibited wide absorption, strong fluorescence, and pH-dependent behavior. The high fluorescence of CPAP-CDs was quenching in the presence of the nitrite ion in a concentration-dependent manner. The detection limit was as low as 6.6 nM with a wide linear detection range of 2 μM − 1 mM. Diazotization between the NO2 − ion and CPAP-CDs resulted in the aggregation of CPAP-CDs and aggregation-induced emission quenching. The as-designed method was tested further with different water samples, such as tap, drinking, and seawater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heteroatom
Fluorescence
Nitrite ion
Diazotization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of NO2 − in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) ( 2020R1A4A4079954 ) and Priority Research Centers Program (MOE) ( 2021R1A6A1A03038858 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: HRTEM image), (B) particle size distribution, (C) XRD pattern, and (D) FTIR spectrum of CPAP-CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectra of CPAP-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) O1s, (D) B1s, and (E) N1s for CPAP-CDs. XPS analysis was performed using the vacuum dried solid under 60 °C.

(A) Spectra of CPAP-CDs, blank line: UV–visible absorbance, peaks at 283 and 373 nm; red line: PL emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm; blue dot line: PL excitation spectra at the emission wavelength of 421 nm. The inset shows photographs of the CPAP-CDs under visible light (left) and UV light (right). (B) PL emission spectra of CPAP-CDs with different excitation wavelengths.

(A) The PL emission, (B) relative PL emission (at 421 nm), (C) absorbance, and (D) relative UV−visible absorbance spectra (at 444 nm) of CPAP-CDs in the presence of different analytes, respectively. Inset: the photographs of CPAP-CDs in the presence of NO2 − under UV light (B) and visible light (D). [CPAP-CDs] = 0.22 mg/mL, pH = 3, and λex = 370 nm.
Influence of pH on the PL emission intensity at 421 nm of CPAP-CDs in the absence and presence of 1 mM NO2 − in various pH solutions. [CPAP-CDs] = 0.22 mg/mL and λex = 370 nm.

(A) Variation of the PL emission intensities of CPAP-CDs with different NO2 − concentrations. (B) Relative PL emission intensities of CPAP-CDs vs. [NO2 −]. (C) The linear dynamic range of the relative PL emission intensity vs. [NO2 −] from 2 − 1000 μM. (D) The linear relationships at the low concentration range from 0 − 0.5 μM. [CPAP-CDs] = 0.22 mg/mL, pH = 3, and λex = 370 nm.
The relative PL emission spectra of the CPAP-CDs-NO2 − system in the presence of different anions. [CPAP-CDs] = 0.22 mg/mL, [NO2 −/other anions] = 500 μM, pH = 3, and λex = 370 nm.
(A) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CPAP-CDs and CPAP-CDs-NO2 −, respectively. [CPAP-CDs] = 0.22 mg/mL, [NO2 −] = 1 mM, pH = 3, and λex = 375 nm.

Zeta potential values for (A) CPAP-CDs and (B) CPAP-CDs-NO2 − system, respectively. [CPAP-CDs] = 0.22 mg/mL, [NO2 −] = 1 mM, and pH = 3. (C) FTIR spectra of CPAP-CDs in the absence and presence of NO2 −.
Schematic illustration of a suggested fluorescence quenching mechanism of CPAP-CDs in the presence of NO2 − in solutions (pH = 3) (not to scale).
Detection of the NO2 − concentration in water obtained from different sources using the proposed method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Designing an intriguingly fluorescent N, B-doped carbon dots based fluorescent probe for selective detection of NO2 − ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan 44610, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan 44610, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and boron doped carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment, and emit bright blue fluorescence. The sensing platform for NO2 − detection was based on the diazo coupling reaction and the aggregation-induced emission quenching.

Low-cost nitrogen and boron-doped carbon nanodots (CPAP-CDs) with a high quantum yield (64.07%) were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal treatment. The obtained CPAP-CDs exhibited wide absorption, strong fluorescence, and pH-dependent behavior. The high fluorescence of CPAP-CDs was quenching in the presence of the nitrite ion in a concentration-dependent manner. The detection limit was as low as 6.6 nM with a wide linear detection range of 2 μM − 1 mM. Diazotization between the NO2 − ion and CPAP-CDs resulted in the aggregation of CPAP-CDs and aggregation-induced emission quenching. The as-designed method was tested further with different water samples, such as tap, drinking, and seawater.

ce:section.ce:para: The nitrite ion (NO2 −) is a significant nitrogen compound that plays a significant role in the nitrogen cycle. NO2 − ions are usually used as fertilizers for plants [1]. NO2 − ions are also used extensively as food additives to improve the presentation, flavors, and storage of food [2]. Nevertheless, NO2 − is considered an inorganic contaminant in drinking water. An excessive intake can lead to health problems, such as blue baby syndrome, hypertension, and esophageal cancer [3-5]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has already prescribed that the maximum content of nitrite in drinking water could not over 65 μM (or 3 mg/L) [6]. Therefore, monitoring the NO2 − concentration in drinking water and the living environment has become vital. Various analytical techniques have been used for NO2 − ion detection, including ions chromatography method [7], electrochemistry [8], spectrophotometry [9], chemiluminescent analysis [10], surface enhanced raman scattering spectroscopy [11], and fluorescence sensing methods [12,13]. The expensive and complicated instruments, toxic raw materials and solvents, and complicated synthetic pathways of some detection methods have highlighted the need for a NO2 − detecting method that is nontoxic, simple in operation, involves low-cost synthesis, with high selectivity, and excellent sensitivity.

Recently, different synthetic processes for carbon dots have been explored, such as chemical exfoliation, electrochemical synthesis, ultrasonic preparation, microwave method, and hydrothermal processes [14,15]. Compared to the conventional fluorescent probes, carbon dots have some unique properties, such as low-cost, non-toxic, easy synthesis, chemically resistant, water-soluble, photostability, wide absorption range, tunable emission, high quantum yield, high selectivity and sensitivity [16-20]. Carbon dots have been employed successfully for electrochemistry, photocatalysts, bioimaging, biosensing, intelligent drug delivery, and environmental applications assigning to their unique physical and chemical properties [21-25]. The sensing process is usually observed through the fluorescence quenching or enhancement in the presence of analytes. The sensing mechanisms mainly involve photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, and dynamic quenching [26,27].

In this study, nitrogen and boron-doped high quantum yield carbon dots (CPAP-CDs) with a mean particle diameter of 3.07 nm were synthesized through the facile hydrothermal treatment. The obtained CPAP-CDs illustrated wide absorption and strong fluorescence in solutions with a quantum yield as high as 64.07%. Physical and chemical characterization showed that the CPAP-CDs contain the nitrogen and boron atoms as heteroatoms at the carbon core and amino and boronic acid functional groups at the surface, which contributed to the increase of fluorescence emission intensity and excellent selectivity of as-obtained CDs towards NO2 − ions. The presence of the hetero atoms would modify the electronic state of the CPAP-CDs and would create more surface defects. Presence of defects would modify the radiative recombination and that would result in increment of fluorescence emission intensity of CPAP-CDs [28,29]. Also the presence of heteroatoms at the core and surface functional groups modified the optoelectronic property, physical and chemical properties, surface and local chemical reactivity of the obtained carbon dots [29]. In this experiment, the bright photoluminescence (PL) emissions of CPAP-CDs have been quenching with the presence of NO2 −. The detection limit was calculated to be as low as 6.6 nM, which is one of the lowest values among ever reported (Table S1). A diazotization reaction between NO2 − and the surface amino group of the obtained CPAP-CDs resulted in aggregation of the CPAP-CDs that quenched the inherent fluorescence. The effects of the synthetic conditions, pH, and interference of other anions for carbon dots were studied in detail. As a determination probe, the CPAP-CDs had enormous potential for the selective and sensitive sensing of NO2 − in water. The resulting carbon dots were used to analyze different water samples, including tap water, drinking water, and seawater.
The experimental section of the Electronic Supporting Information (ESI) provides the detailed information for the materials and characterization.

To synthesize the CPAP-CDs, 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (3-CPBA, 33 mg) and 3-aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate (3-APBA·H2O, 31 mg) were both put into 12 mL of deionized water (DW), and the mixture was sonication for five minutes to prepare a homogenous solution. Afterwards, the mixed solutions were transmitted to an autoclave with 50 mL and transferred under 240 °C for 4 h for farther reactions. After finishing reaction and the temperature cooled down, the CPAP-CDs solutions were obtained through centrifuging with 10000 rpm for 15 min and depurated via the dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 4 h to eliminate the unreacted regents. The obtained yellowy CPAP-CDs solutions were conserved at room temperature for next characterization. Figure S1 exhibited the optimizations of the precursor ratio and reaction temperature to achieve the strongest PL emission intensity and the absorbance for CPAP-CDs.

To measure the NO2 − amount, diverse concentrations of NO2 − were added to 2 mL of acetic acid (HAc) and sodium acetate (NaAc) solutions (pH = 3.0) containing 0.22 mg/mL CPAP-CDs. After constant stirring for 2 h at room temperature, the PL emission intensity of each sample was detected at 421 nm under exciting with 370 nm. All photoluminescence experiments were measured using a 2.5 nm excitation slit and a 5 nm emission slit. The selectivity detections of NO2 − towards the interfering chemicals were measured in a similar manner mentioned above, with the interfering chemicals added to the solutions at a concentration ratio of 1:1.

Fig. 1 A illustrated the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as-obtained CPAP-CDs. The nanoparticles were distributed with an average particle diameter of 3.07 nm, as shown in Fig. 1B. A further close observation from a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 1A) revealed that the lattice fringe spacing of 0.245 nm, which is corresponding to the C(100) plane of graphitized carbon structure [30,31]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 1C revealed two obvious wide single diffraction peaks 2θ approximately located at around 21° and 43°, which are assigned to disordered carbon atoms with a high degree of C(002) and C(100) planes for the hexagonal graphite structure, respectively [32]. The d-spacing of the obtained CPAP-CDs was calculated using Bragg’s equation (1) based on the position of the C(002)/C(100) reflection peak [33].
(1)
n λ = 2 d s i n θ

Where, n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of incident X-ray (λ = 1.54 Å) and θ is the position of the C(002)/C(100) peak. The d-spacing of CPAP-CDs was approximately 0.33 nm and 0.22 nm for the C(002) and C(100) peaks, respectively. On the other hand, the XRD peak of the CPAP-CDs was broadened due to the small sized CPAP-CDs.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectroscopies are applied to examine the elemental compositions and surface functional groups of the resulting nanoparticles, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 3455, 3346–3228, 1655, 1553, 1399, 1358, 1294, 1161, and 1070 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH, −NH2, CO, CC, BO, CN, CO, BOH, and CH bonds, respectively (Fig. 1D) [16,34]. As shown in Fig. 2 , the XPS spectra showed that CPAP-CDs were comprised of carbon, oxygen, boron, and nitrogen. The C1s XPS spectrum of the CPAP-CDs could be fitted to four peaks centered at ∼282.18, ∼282.68, ∼284.17, and ∼286.80 eV, which were assigned to CB, CC/CC, CO, and NCO bonds, respectively [35]. Deconvolution of the O1s XPS spectrum were exhibited three peaks at ∼529.52, ∼530.43, and ∼531.72 eV, corresponding to CO, CO/NO, and BO, separately [36]. Corresponding to the B 1 s spectra showed peaks centered around ∼189.69 and ∼191.24 eV, originated from BC and BO, separately [37]. Fitting of the N1s XPS spectra revealed peaks centered around ∼397.54 and ∼399.20 eV, which were assigned to NC and NH, separately [38]. Overall, the as-obtained CPAP-CDs might have NH2, −COOH, and NCO functional groups.

UV–visible absorbance and fluorescent spectra have been carried out to inspect the properties of the obtained CPAP-CDs. Observed from Fig. 3 A, the UV−visible absorbance spectrum of CPAP-CDs revealed two distinct absorption peaks at 283 and 373 nm, could be attributable to π–π* electronic transitions of the aromatic double carbon groups (CC) and the n–π* electronic transitions of carbonyl groups (CO), respectively [39,40]. The CPAP-CDs aqueous solutions emitted strong blue emission under ultraviolet light. While excited at 370 nm, the CPAP-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence at the emission peak of 421 nm. The inset in Fig. 3A shows that the CPAP-CDs solutions were yellowish under sunlight lamp and glowing strong blue emission within ultraviolet lamp. The PL excitation spectra of the CPAP-CDs exhibited the maximum PL emission peak at 421 nm with excited at 370 nm. When excitation wavelength was increased from 290 to 400 nm in 10 nm increments (Fig. 3B), the fluorescence spectrum revealed the exciting wavelength dependent photoluminescence phenomenon. This behavior has been assigned to the functional group of the CPAP-CDs on the surface [16,41]. The calculated quantum yield was 64.07% by employing aqueous quinine sulfate (QS, in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the chemical reference substance (Figure S2). The stability of CPAP-CDs was explored with respect to the PL emission intensity over 120 days (Figure S3). The results exhibited the long-term stability and usability of CPAP-CDs. As well, the stabilities of the CPAP-CDs in various amount of sodium chloride aqueous have been explored (Figure S4). These results revealed no prominent fluorescence changes, indicating the properties of the as-obtained CPAP-CDs are considerably stable, even though under the experimental conditions of high ionic concentrations. The CPAP-CDs were responsive towards the pH of the medium. Formerly, it has published that the hydrophilicities and dispersions of carbon nanodots increased under the low pH conditions [22,42]. The PL emission decreased significantly with increasing the pH can be ascribe to the deprotonation of the surface functional groups of CDs, leading to the agglomeration of CDs (Figure S5) [43].

The effects of the fluorescence response and UV−visible absorbance of the as-obtained CPAP-CDs exposed to various anions [NO2 −, NO3 −, B4O7 2−, CO3 2−, S2−, HPO4 2−, N3 −, SCN−, I−, Br−, Cl−, F−, Ac−] were investigated. Fig. 4 shows the PL emission and UV−visible absorption spectra of the CPAP-CDs in the presence of different anions. No other anions except for NO2 − caused significant changes (photograph inset Fig. 4B). The maximum PL quenching was obtained after 2 h (Figure S6). The PL emission spectra showed that in the presence of NO2 −, the position of the CPAP-CDs PL emission peak was not altered notably. On the other hand, the UV−visible absorbance spectrum showed that in the presence of NO2 −, a new absorption appeared around 444 nm, and the CPAP-CDs peaks showed a redshift along with an increase in the absorbance intensity. The color of the CPAP-CDs solutions with the addition of NO2 − changed from light yellow to dark yellow (photograph inset Fig. 4D).

The pH of the medium solution plays an important role during sensing applications. Demonstrated as Fig. 5 , the photoluminescence intensities of CPAP-CDs in the absence and presence of NO2 − were measured at various pH from 3 to 10. The more obvious quenching effect can be observed with adding the same concentration of the aqueous nitrite in lower pH solutions. Because nitrous acid is a weak acid, hydrolysis reaction of nitrite ion exists in water. The hydrolysis reaction of nitrite ion was reinforced under the acidic condition. Therefore, the content of nitrous acid in aqueous solution increased, which enhanced the effect of fluorescence quenching due to the higher activity of nitrous acid than nitrite ion [44,45]. All NO2 − sensing experimental conditions were conducted at pH = 3.

The fluorescence quenching of CPAP-CDs by NO2 − was examined by the quantitative detection of NO2 −. Fig. 6 reveals that the PL quenching was dependent on the NO2 − concentration. The photoluminescence intensities of the CPAP-CDs decreased within gradually increasing NO2 − concentration. The relative photoluminescence intensities (I0/I) at 421 nm were detected as the functions of the NO2 − amount ([NO2 −]) from the range of 0–1000 μM. The photoluminescence of CPAP-CDs was quenched in the presence of nitrite has been depicted using a Stern-Volmer plot, as described below:
(2)
I 0
I
= 1 + K
Q

Where, K is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is the NO2 − amount. I0 and I denote the photoluminescence intensities of CPAP-CDs in the absence and presence of NO2 −, respectively. The plot of I0/I versus [NO2 −] was linear over the range, 2–1000 μM (R2 = 0.978). At the low linear detection range of 0–-0.5 μM, a linear detection relation was obtained (R2 = 0.988). The LOD (3σ/k) was examined to be 6.6 nM, indicating that CPAP-CDs could be a sensitive probe for NO2 − detection.

Fig. 7 showed that at a NO2 − concentration ratio of 1:1, with other anions, there was no important change in the PL emission intensity of the CPAP-CDs-NO2 − system. Compared to other studies (Table S1), these results highlight the significantly important detection limit of NO2 − with an impressive quantum yield of the probe.

NO2 − can quench the fluorescence of the CPAP-CDs obtained in this measurement, which may be related to the amino groups on the surface of CPAP-CDs. To further explore the reasons for the quenching of CPAP-CDs by nitrite ions, the fluorescence lifetime of the probe and probe-analyte system were performed. The calculation of average fluorescence lifetime (
τ
av
)
is shown in the supporting information. The decay profile showed that
τ
av
value of the CPAP-CDs changed from 9.05 ns to 5.08 ns in the presence of NO2 − (Fig. 8 ). The results indicated that dynamic quenching might occur [46]. The calculated zeta potentials of the CPAP-CDs and CPAP-CDs-NO2 − were −11.3 mV and 10.4 mV, respectively (Fig. 9 A and B). The significant variation of zeta potential under experimental conditions indicated the strong interactions between CPAP-CDs and NO2 − ions.

The absorption peak of CPAP-CDs after participating in the coupling reaction with NO2 − exhibited a 71 nm redshift (from 373 to 444 nm), which was attributed to the formation of a diazo group (N = N) via a diazo coupling reaction [47-49]. The color change of the CPAP-CDs solutions arose from the formation of azo compounds. A number of previous articles have verified that NO2 − ions could diazotize primary arylamines to form diazotized compound in strongly acidic conditions [50]. The FTIR spectra of CPAP-CDs after reacting with NO2 − via the diazo coupling reaction was also examined (Fig. 9C).

FTIR spectroscopy showed that the aromatic amine peak at around 3346–3228 cm−1 had almost vanished with the concomitant appearance of a new wavenumber peak at 1698 cm−1, assigning to the generation of the azo groups [51]. Moreover, the peak strength of the surface functional groups changed significantly. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s of CPAP-CDs-NO2 − (Figure S7) revealed the presence of a =N− peak (397.33 eV) [52,53], indicating successful diazotization between the CPAP-CDs and NO2 −. Moreover, TEM showed that CPAP-CDs aggregated in presence of NO2 − (Figure S8). The hydrodynamic diameter of CPAP-CDs changed from 37.84 nm to 1446 nm after adding 1 mM NO2 − (Figure S9). The DLS data indicated that CPAP-CDs aggregated in the presence of NO2 − [12]. Furthermore, NO2 −-induced fluorescence quenching was unrecoverable in the addition of various cationic ions (Figure S10), exhibiting the strong interactions between NO2 − and the CPAP-CDs [54]. We proposed that the strong interactions of NO2 − with −NH2 group on the surface of the CPAP-CDs resulted in aggregation of the CPAP-CDs, which ultimately leaded to an aggregation-induced PL emission quenching (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NO2 − in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The designed CPAP-CDs were tested for their ability to determine the NO2 − concentration in various water sources, including tap, drinking, and sea water. The water samples were collecting from the local area, centrifuging, and treating with ultraviolet for the removal of impurity components. Real environmental conditions were mimicked by spiking the water samples with different NO2 − concentrations, and the PL intensity of each sample was measured under the experimental conditions. Table 1 lists the recovery results from the spiked NO2 − concentration in various water sources. These results show that the CPAP-CDs can be used for the selective and sensitive detection of NO2 − in various water sources.

Nitrogen and boron-doped carbon nanodots with highly quantum yields were designed for the selective and sensitive determination of NO2 −. The sensing performance of the obtained CPAP-CDs was examined using the fluorescence “turn off” phenomenon. The aggregation-induced PL emission quenching was ascribed to a diazo coupling reaction between the −NH2 group of the as-obtained carbon dot particles and NO2 − ions in aqueous solutions. The proposed determination probe reached a nanomolar detection limit in low pH solutions. The applicability of the determination probe was assessed using different water samples with reasonable RSD values. The pH-dependent behaviors of the CPAP-CDs provided a platform for NO2 − measurement to a satisfactory level. The proposal has the potential to be extended to the sensitive and selective determination of NO2 − in different water environments.

Linlin Wang: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Jayasmita Jana: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Data curation. Jin Suk Chung: Validation, Project administration. Won Mook Choi: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Seung Hyun Hur: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (2020R1A4A4079954) and Priority Research Centers Program (MOE)(2021R1A6A1A03038858).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120657.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.O. Hall, Nitrate- and nitrite-accumulating plants, Veterinary Toxicology (2018) 941-946.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-mode sensor for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of nitrite in hams based on carbon dots-neutral red system

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of nitrates in drinking water: maternal exposure and adverse reproductive and developmental outcomes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M. Manassaram, L.C. Backer, D.M. Moll, A review of nitrates in drinking water: maternal exposure and adverse reproductive and developmental outcomes, Environmental Health Perspectives 114 (2006) 320-327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-Nitrosodimethylamine as a hazardous chemical toxicant in drinking water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Sharma, S. Manu, N-Nitrosodimethylamine as a hazardous chemical toxicant in drinking water, International Research Journal of Pharmacy 3 (2012) 60-65.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visual detection of nitrite in sausage based on a ratiometric fluorescent system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, Y. Shi, X. Hu, Z. Li, X. Huang, M. Holmes, et al., Visual detection of nitrite in sausage based on a ratiometric fluorescent system Food Control 106 (2019) 106704.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: WHO, Guidelines for drinking-water quality: incorporating first addendum. Vol. 1, Recommendations. – 3rd ed., Geneva, Switzerland: World Health Organization (2008).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HPLC-DAD Determination of Nitrite and Nitrate in Human Saliva Utilizing a Phosphatidylcholine Column

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Tatarczak-Michalewska, J. Flieger, J. Kawka, W. Płaziński, W. Flieger, E. Blicharska, et al., HPLC-DAD Determination of Nitrite and Nitrate in Human Saliva Utilizing a Phosphatidylcholine Column, Molecules 24 (2019) 1754.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface engineering of carbon fiber paper toward exceptionally high-performance and stable electrochemical nitrite sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhu, Y. Zhang, J. Gong, Y. Ma, J. Sun, T. Li, et al., Surface engineering of carbon fiber paper toward exceptionally high-performance and stable electrochemical nitrite sensing, ACS Sensors 4 (2019) 2980-2987.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Poly (3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) hollow microflowers and their application for nitrite sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-H. Cheng, C.-W. Kung, L.-Y. Chou, R. Vittal, K.-C. Ho, Poly (3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) hollow microflowers and their application for nitrite sensing, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 192 (2014) 762-768.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive and selective electrochemiluminescent detection of nitrite using dual-stabilizer-capped CdTe quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yin, Q. Chen, H. Song, M. Yang, H. Wang, Sensitive and selective electrochemiluminescent detection of nitrite using dual-stabilizer-capped CdTe quantum dots, Electrochemistry communications 34 (2013) 81-85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diazo-reaction-based SERS substrates for detection of nitrite in saliva

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yang, Y. Xiang, X. Guo, Y. Wu, Y. Wen, H. Yang, Diazo-reaction-based SERS substrates for detection of nitrite in saliva, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 271 (2018) 118-121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and phosphorus doped polymer carbon dots as a sensitive cellular mapping probe of nitrite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Jiang, M. Lin, T. Yang, R.S. Li, C.Z. Huang, J. Wang, et al., Nitrogen and phosphorus doped polymer carbon dots as a sensitive cellular mapping probe of nitrite, Journal of Materials Chemistry B 7 (2019) 2074-2080.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The flexibility of side chains for adjusting the emission of 4-aryl-1,8-naphthalimides in aggregation: Spectral study and cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Luo, Y. Qu, Y. Zhang, Y. Wu, W. Yuan, L. Wang, The flexibility of side chains for adjusting the emission of 4-aryl-1,8-naphthalimides in aggregation: Spectral study and cell imaging, Chemical Engineering Journal 415 (2021) 129095.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: A mystic star in the world of nanoscience

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Gayen, S. Palchoudhury, J. Chowdhury, Carbon dots: A mystic star in the world of nanoscience. , Journal of Nanomaterials 2019 (2019) 3451307.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Li, T. Chen, J.J. Gooding, J. Liu, Review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing, ACS Sensors 4 (2019) 1732-1748.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor Emitting N-Doped Carbon Dots Derived from Ascorbic Acid and Phenylenediamine Precursors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural evolution of graphene quantum dots during thermal decomposition of citric acid and the corresponding photoluminescence
